what_manipulated: temporal framing of political message (past-focused vs. future-focused appeal to nostalgia)
what_measured: agreement with or support for political ideas; disagreement reduction between groups
experiment: Nine separate studies test whether communicating political ideas with past-focused framing increases support among conservative units. Studies 1-6 manipulate message framing (past vs. future) and measure support across diverse political topics. Studies measure agreement ratings, support indices. Study 7 manipulates cognitive processing mode to test when effect occurs. Study 8 tests directional asymmetry. Study 9 synthesizes results via meta-analysis. Comparisons are between-subjects; analysis tests main effects, mediating mechanisms (nostalgia), and interactions with processing depth.

what_manipulated: temporal focus (bringing to mind past self vs. future self)
what_measured: perceived rationality-vs.-emotionality of the self; choice of enrichment activity; self-control task performance
experiment: Six studies examine how time orientation affects trait attribution and behavior. Studies 1a-1c measure units' trait ratings of their past vs. future selves on rationality and emotionality dimensions. Study 2 tests whether asymmetry appears when rating others or is unique to self-judgment. Studies 3-4 present behavioral outcomes following the temporal manipulation: enrichment preference (study 3) and task performance (study 4) with context variation (performance labeled as measuring control vs. enjoyment). Analysis uses ratings and behavioral measures to test the asymmetry hypothesis and consequences.

what_manipulated: none (methodological review; no direct manipulation)
what_measured: meta-analytic indicators of selection bias and reporting irregularities in prior literature
experiment: A critical commentary and methodological reassessment of the money-priming literature. Authors apply meta-analytic tools (publication bias, p-curve, effect-size heterogeneity) to assess whether effect irregularities indicate selection bias or p-hacking. Analysis examines both published replications showing effects and preregistered replications showing null results to evaluate which pattern is more consistent with true effects versus biased reporting.

what_manipulated: environmental setup and decision-making context in agent-based learning tasks; simulations vary key agent and environment attributes
what_measured: formation of stable false beliefs despite continued experience; learning accuracy
experiment: A conceptual review identifies mechanisms of learning traps, followed by computational simulations demonstrating how selective attention interaction with environment properties produces false-belief stability. Then, a series of experiments presents units with dynamic environments and observes belief formation over time. Units make decisions and receive feedback; analysis tests whether stable false beliefs emerge despite disconfirming experience. Experiments test robustness against various hypothetical interventions. Statistical tests examine frequency and persistence of belief formation.

what_manipulated: experience of disrespect (recalled or behavioral context); endorsement of cynical beliefs; behavioral expression of disrespect toward others
what_measured: cynicism (belief in moral bankruptcy of people); experience of disrespect; treatment of others with disrespect
experiment: Study 1 is a cross-national survey showing association between reported disrespect and cynicism. Study 2 uses longitudinal data across 4 years to test bidirectional prediction. Studies 3-5 are preregistered experiments: Study 3 primes memory of disrespect experiences and measures subsequent cynicism; Studies 4-5 manipulate cynicism endorsement and measure whether others treat units disrespectfully in response. Study 6 is a weeklong diary study tracking daily experience of disrespect and cynical beliefs. Analysis tests vicious-cycle hypothesis via cross-lagged predictions, experimental manipulation, and mediation.

what_manipulated: none (observational; measured political ideology)
what_measured: anterior cingulate cortex activity (conflict-detection neural response); political ideology
experiment: Units with varying political ideologies complete tasks designed to elicit conflict or error responses while event-related potentials are recorded. Analysis measures anterior cingulate activity (a conflict-related component) and tests correlation with self-reported political ideology. Comparison is observational: units naturally differ in ideology; analysis reveals whether neural sensitivity to task conflict covaries with ideology.

what_manipulated: visual cue availability (self-refilling bowl obscuring consumption volume vs. normal bowl with accurate visual cue)
what_measured: food intake volume; subjective intake estimation; satiety perception
experiment: A between-subjects experiment assigns units to eat soup from either normal bowls (accurate visual cue) or self-refilling bowls (biased visual cue obscuring true consumption). Units eat ad libitum. Outcome measures include actual consumption volume (weighed), subjective estimates of intake, and satiety rating. Analysis compares intake and subjective ratings between visibility conditions via ANOVAs; also tests whether effects vary by BMI.

what_manipulated: none (observational; age group)
what_measured: socio-cognitive abilities (theory of mind, metacognition); socio-affective abilities (empathy, compassion); factual reasoning
experiment: Units of varying ages complete a naturalistic task designed to measure multiple facets of social understanding simultaneously. Analysis separates socio-cognitive components (theory of mind, metacognition about social content) from socio-affective components (empathy, compassion ratings) and compares to non-social metacognition and reasoning. Comparisons are observational across age groups. Statistical tests assess age-related differences for each component.

what_manipulated: none (observational; participant age)
what_measured: age estimate of ambiguous face image (younger vs. older interpretation)
experiment: Units of varying ages view a young/old lady ambiguous figure briefly and estimate the age depicted. Outcomes are age estimates. Analysis compares estimates across participant ages to test whether units interpret the image as consistent with their own age group. Comparison is observational across age groups.

what_manipulated: eccentricity (distance from fixation point) and facial expression valence (happy, fearful, neutral)
what_measured: valence-recognition accuracy; eye gaze position
experiment: Units perform a facial valence judgment task while gaze is tracked. Faces appear at varying eccentricities in the parafoveal region (up to 4 degrees). Each face displays positive, negative, or neutral emotion. Units judge emotional valence while gaze location is recorded. Analysis measures recognition accuracy across eccentricities and valences. Between-subjects analysis tests whether performance decreases differently for different emotion types as distance from fixation increases. Tests include examination of whether effects are due to speed-accuracy tradeoffs or response biases.

what_manipulated: exposure to online game teaching misinformation production techniques (gameplay vs. no gameplay or delayed gameplay)
what_measured: ability to spot and resist misinformation; misinformation detection accuracy
experiment: A large-scale pre-post design assigns units to play an online browser game where they learn six misinformation-production techniques (polarization, emotion-invoking, conspiracy, trolling, blame-deflecting, impersonation). The control condition receives no gameplay or delayed gameplay. Outcome measures include accuracy on misinformation detection tasks pre and post gameplay. Analysis tests whether gameplay improves detection ability and whether effects vary by demographic factors (education, age, political ideology, cognitive style).

what_manipulated: syntactic category structure in three-word sequences (repeated vs. non-repeated within syntax; syntactically licit vs. illicit patterns)
what_measured: pattern detection ability; learning of repetition structure in sequences
experiment: Units listen to sequences of three words and learn whether the sequences follow simple repetition patterns (e.g., word 1 and word 2 both from the same syntactic category). Sequences are constructed so the target pattern is either consistent or inconsistent with possible syntactic structures. Units are instructed to look for patterns. Analysis measures learning success (whether units detect the pattern) via performance metrics or explicit reports. Tests compare learning success when patterns align with syntactic structures versus when they conflict with syntax.

what_manipulated: linguistic framing of survey question (personal identity phrasing e.g., "being a voter" vs. behavioral phrasing e.g., "voting")
what_measured: voter registration interest; actual voting behavior; voter turnout
experiment: Three randomized experiments vary survey phrasing. Experiment 1 presents questions about voting with identity-focused vs. behavior-focused language and measures interest in registering to vote. Experiments 2-3 manipulate phrasing in actual election contexts and measure voter turnout using official state voting records. Analysis compares registration interest and turnout rates between phrasing conditions.

what_manipulated: social class (measured via personal report; also manipulated via experimental framing in later studies)
what_measured: unethical behavior indicators (traffic violations, unethical choices, theft, lying, cheating, endorsement of unethical conduct)
experiment: Studies 1-2 are observational, comparing traffic violation frequency across social classes. Studies 3-7 are between-subjects experiments: units are assigned to high or low social class conditions or respond as themselves; outcomes measure unethical behaviors (taking valued goods, lying in negotiation, cheating for prizes, endorsing workplace unethical conduct). Mediator analysis tests role of attitudes toward greed. Comparisons test main effects of social class on unethical behavior.

what_manipulated: presence of noise in auditory sentence input (sentences with/without word errors); semantic prior expectation compatibility
what_measured: sentence interpretation (word choice for ambiguous meaning); comprehension accuracy
experiment: Units listen to and interpret three-word sentences. Manipulation varies the semantic plausibility of alternative interpretations and the presence/type of input errors (insertions vs. deletions from intended utterance). Outcomes measure which interpretation units select or how units resolve ambiguity. Analysis tests whether interpretations follow Bayesian predictions: units should favor interpretations close (in edit distance) to the input if semantically plausible, and asymmetry between insertion and deletion error types is predicted by size principle.

what_manipulated: intentionality of harm (intentional vs. unintentional action causing damage); task choice availability (blaming/condemning/punishing vs. other appealing alternatives)
what_measured: motivation to blame (choice persistence, effort, rate, duration); blame and condemnation intensity; harm magnitude estimation
experiment: A preliminary experiment compares harm-magnitude overestimation for intentional vs. unintentional harms. Experiments 1-2 measure motivation to blame using task-choice paradigms: units are offered choices between blaming-related tasks and other appealing activities under intentional-harm vs. unintentional-harm scenarios. Effort, persistence, rate, and duration of blaming behavior are recorded. Analysis tests whether units preferentially choose and persist with blaming when harms are perceived as intentional versus unintentional.

what_manipulated: option to intervene as a third party in a conflict (interval possible vs. not possible); risk to third party from intervention (high vs. low)
what_measured: cooperation rate among disputants; third-party intervention rate; outcome payoffs
experiment: Six experiments use the Peacemaker Game paradigm: two units act as disputants choosing cooperation vs. competition; a third unit chooses whether to intervene (if available). Intervention changes game payoffs and introduces risk to the intervener. Between-subjects variations manipulate presence of third-party option and risk level. Outcomes measure cooperation frequency among disputants, intervention frequency, and resulting payoffs. Analysis tests whether mere availability of third-party intervention affects cooperation and whether reducing intervention risk increases intervention rates and mutual welfare.

what_manipulated: postural expansiveness vs. contraction (in field interactions and static photos)
what_measured: romantic attraction (speed-dating yes/no response); mate selection in online-dating context
experiment: Field study 1 (speed dating): units interact briefly while nonverbal behavior is coded; postural expansiveness is extracted; outcome is whether dating partner reciprocates interest (yes/no). Field experiment 2 (online dating): photographs vary in whether the subject displays expanded vs. contracted posture; outcome is selection frequency by mate-seekers. Mediation analysis tests whether dominance perception explains the effect of expansiveness on selection.

what_manipulated: economic inequality level (high vs. low, measured via state-level data in study 1; manipulated experimentally in study 2 via portrayal of inequality in participants' home states)
what_measured: generosity (real-stakes giving amount); income level
experiment: Study 1 is observational: units of varying income in states of high vs. low inequality are offered a real giving opportunity; generosity (actual money allocated) is measured and correlated with income and inequality level. Study 2 is a between-subjects experiment: inequality in units' home states is described as high or low (experimental manipulation); units then face a giving opportunity. Outcomes measure actual giving by income level under high vs. low inequality conditions.

what_manipulated: patient race (black vs. white) in medical scenarios; measured endorsement of false biological-difference beliefs
what_measured: pain rating; treatment recommendation accuracy; endorsement of false biological beliefs
experiment: Study 1 measures laypersons' endorsement of false beliefs about racial biological differences and correlates this with pain ratings assigned to black vs. white targets in vignettes. Study 2 extends to medical trainees: endorsement of false beliefs is measured; medical students/residents rate pain for black vs. white patients and make treatment recommendations. Analysis tests association between belief endorsement and pain-rating bias, and whether this mediates treatment-recommendation errors.
